content:
# Judges Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_269.jpg, JDG_page_270.jpg

1. Now these are the nations that the LORD left, to test Israel by them, that is, all in Israel who had not experienced any of the wars in Canaan.

2. It was only in order that the generations of the people of Israel might know war, to teach war to those who had not known it before.

3. These are the nations: the five lords of the Philistines and all the Canaanites and the Sidonians and the Hivites who lived on Mount Lebanon, from Mount Baal-hermon as far as Lebo-hamath.

4. They were for the testing of Israel, to know whether Israel would obey the commandments of the LORD, which he commanded their fathers by the hand of Moses.

5. So the people of Israel lived among the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites.

6. And their daughters they took to themselves for wives, and their own daughters they gave to their sons, and they served their gods.

7. And the people of Israel did what was evil in the sight of the LORD. They forgot the LORD their God and served the Baals and the Asheroth.

8. Therefore the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia. And the people of Israel served Cushan-rishathaim eight years.

9. But when the people of Israel cried out to the LORD, the LORD raised up a deliverer for the people of Israel, who saved them, Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother.

10. The Spirit of the LORD was upon him, and he judged Israel. He went out to war, and the LORD gave Cushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia into his hand. And his hand prevailed over Cushan-rishathaim.

11. So the land had rest forty years. Then Othniel the son of Kenaz died.

12. And the people of Israel again did what was evil in the sight of the LORD, and the LORD strengthened Eglon the king of Moab against Israel, because they had done what was evil in the sight of the LORD.

13. He gathered to himself the Ammonites and the Amalekites, and went and defeated Israel. And they took possession of the city of palms.

14. And the people of Israel served Eglon the king of Moab eighteen years.

15. Then the people of Israel cried out to the LORD, and the LORD raised up for them a deliverer, Ehud, the son of Gera, the Benjaminite, a left-handed man. The people of Israel sent tribute by him to Eglon the king of Moab.

16. And Ehud made for himself a sword with two edges, a cubit in length, and he strapped it on his right thigh under his clothes.

17. And he presented the tribute to Eglon king of Moab. Now Eglon was a very fat man.

18. And when Ehud had finished presenting the tribute, he sent away the people who carried the tribute.

19. But he himself turned back at the idols near Gilgal and said, "I have a secret message for you, O king." And he commanded, "Silence." And all his attendants went out from his presence.

20. And Ehud came to him as he was sitting alone in his cool roof chamber. And Ehud said, "I have a message from God for you." And he arose from his seat.

21. And Ehud reached with his left hand, took the sword from his right thigh, and thrust it into his belly.

22. And the hilt also went in after the blade, and the fat closed over the blade, for he did not pull the sword out of his belly; and the dagger came out behind.

23. Then Ehud went out into the porch and closed the doors of the roof chamber behind him and locked them.

24. When he had gone, the servants came, and when they saw that the doors of the roof chamber were locked, they thought, "Surely he is relieving himself in the cool chamber."

25. And they waited till they were embarrassed. But when he still did not open the doors of the roof chamber, they took the key and opened them, and there lay their lord dead on the floor.

26. Ehud escaped while they delayed, and he passed beyond the idols and escaped to Seirah.

27. When he arrived, he sounded the trumpet in the hill country of Ephraim. Then the people of Israel went down with him from the hill country, and he was their leader.

28. And he said to them, "Follow after me, for the LORD has given your enemies the Moabites into your hand." So they went down after him and seized the fords of the Jordan against the Moabites and did not allow anyone to pass over.

29. And they struck down about ten thousand of the Moabites at that time, all strong, able-bodied men; not a man escaped.

30. So Moab was subdued that day under the hand of Israel. And the land had rest for eighty years.

31. After him was Shamgar the son of Anath, who struck down six hundred of the Philistines with an oxgoad, and he also saved Israel.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 3 presents the first three judges in a descending pattern of narrative fullness: Othniel (3 verses, the template), Ehud (16 verses, fully developed), Shamgar (1 verse, the bare minimum). Othniel is the "ideal" judge — the cycle works perfectly. Ehud introduces the dark humor and moral ambiguity that characterize the rest of the book. The Ehud narrative is one of the finest pieces of storytelling in the Hebrew Bible.

**Verse 15 — "Left-handed":** itter yad-y'mino — literally "restricted/bound in his right hand." The irony: Ehud is from the tribe of Benjamin (בנימין), which means "son of the right hand." A left-handed son of the right-hand tribe.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.9 — "deliverer" vs. "rescuer"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a savior for the sons of Yisrael" | moshia l'vnei Yisrael |
| LOGOS | "a deliverer for the people of Israel" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a rescuer to save them" | "Rescuer" for moshia |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. moshia comes from the root ישע (yasha', "to save/deliver"). "Deliverer" captures both the military and theological dimensions. "Rescuer" (PNEUMA) reduces the term to a single event; "savior" (EMET) is technically accurate but imports NT connotations into an OT military context. "Deliverer" is the best balance.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.10 — "The Spirit of the LORD was upon him" vs. "The Spirit of the LORD came upon him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Spirit of YHWH was upon him" | vattehi alav ruach YHWH |
| LOGOS | "The Spirit of the LORD was upon him" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "The Spirit of the LORD came upon him" | "Came upon" for "was upon" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew says vattehi alav — "was upon him" (hayah + al). PNEUMA's "came upon" implies a moment of arrival, while "was upon" describes a state. The distinction matters: the Spirit's presence is described as a condition (being upon), not an event (coming upon). Other formulations in Judges use different verbs for the Spirit's dramatic onset (e.g., tsalach in 14:6, "rushed upon").

**Status:** Minor. State vs. event.

---

### v.15 — "a left-handed man" vs. "a man restricted in his right hand"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a man restricted in his right hand" | ish itter yad-y'mino |
| LOGOS | "a left-handed man" | Standard interpretation |
| PNEUMA | "a left-handed man" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. While EMET's literal rendering preserves the Hebrew idiom, "a man restricted in his right hand" is misleading in English — it suggests a disability rather than left-handedness. The Hebrew phrase itter yad-y'mino is a standard idiom for left-handedness (cf. Judges 20:16, where 700 Benjaminites are described with the same term). The narrative logic confirms it: Ehud uses his left hand (v.21), which is why the sword on his right thigh goes undetected.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET noted for literal value.

---

### v.22 — "the dagger came out behind" vs. "the king's bowels emptied"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it came out the back" | vayyetse haparshedonah |
| LOGOS | "the dagger came out behind" | parshedonah as anal exit |
| PNEUMA | "the king's bowels emptied" | Interprets parshedonah as excretion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS with note. parshedonah is a hapax legomenon — it occurs only here. Scholarly opinion is divided: (a) "it [the blade] came out the back/rear" (the dagger exited posteriorly), (b) "excrement came out" (the bowels voided at death), (c) "he went out to the porch" (Ehud exited). LOGOS's "the dagger came out behind" follows the majority scholarly reading while preserving ambiguity. PNEUMA's "bowels emptied" is one valid interpretation but presented as certain when the Hebrew is not.

**Status:** Minor but textually notable. Hapax legomenon — all translations are interpretive.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA changes state to event | 1 | "Came upon" for "was upon" (v.10) |
| PNEUMA selects one interpretation of ambiguity | 1 | "Bowels emptied" for hapax parshedonah (v.22) |
| Minor lexical choices | 2 | "Rescuer" for moshia (v.9), left-handedness idiom (v.15) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 3's disagreements are relatively minor. The most interesting is v.22 — the hapax parshedonah. When the Hebrew is genuinely uncertain, LOGOS preserves the ambiguity while PNEUMA picks one interpretation. The narrative itself trades in bodily humor and grotesque detail; each translation handles this differently.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
